The operations log is what actually happened, in the order it happened, and is NEVER rewritten or edited — only added to at the end.
A stocktake is a photograph, rewritten whenever it changes. A log is a diary, only ever appended to. They are never merged into one record.
Instructions say what SHOULD happen; the log says what DID happen. Their disagreement is drift, and is the only place drift can be seen.

The manual changes through exactly one door: a fault is raised from below, written into the log, reported upward, the owner decides, and it is amended once for everybody. Nothing changes the way a dish is cooked by any other route.
Nobody ever checks their own work. Whoever made a thing never approves it. The taster never cooked the dish it judges.
If a required check cannot be made, the kitchen does not serve. No checker, no service.
The doctor diagnoses and prescribes but NEVER treats what it finds.
Written instructions travel to a new restaurant unchanged. Records — the stocktake and the log — never travel.
The menu is local: a restaurant may serve FEWER dishes than the chain, never different ones. The recipe itself is never local.
People are named by role, never by person. What goes to the owner is a decision, never a task.
